decisions.issue: Workflow engine architecture: external orchestrator vs custom state machine?
decisions.recommendation: Custom event-sourced state machine using existing forge patterns (JSON + Redis + AgentLoop)
decisions.reasoning: forge-api already has 80% of needed infrastructure. Workflows are minutes-long (not days), running on desktop (not cloud). External orchestrators (Temporal, Prefect) add operational overhead and infrastructure dependencies disproportionate to the actual need. Custom approach gives full control and zero new dependencies.

findings.finding: AgentLoop already supports streaming, tool execution, and blocking-decision pause/resume
findings.detail: The exact patterns needed for per-step workflow execution are already built and tested

findings.finding: EventBus (Redis pub/sub) has 30+ event types and WebSocket integration
SessionManager handles Redis-backed persistent sessions with async locks
findings.detail: Per-step sessions provide clean context boundaries
Temporal needs a server, Prefect needs cloud or server — overkill for desktop app
Lacks user decision gates, multi-session orchestration, forge-core integration

options.name: Prefect
cons: Not ideal for AI agent loops

decisions.resolution_notes: Custom event-sourced state machine accepted. User clarified: NOT JSON files — use Postgres for persistence (see D-002 override). Redis for events/sessions, AgentLoop for LLM execution.

decisions.issue: Persistence layer for workflow state: JSON files vs Postgres vs Redis-only?
decisions.recommendation: JSON files in forge_output/{project}/workflows.json with atomic writes
decisions.reasoning: Consistent with all other forge state (tracker.json, decisions.json, etc.). No new infrastructure needed. forge-core already solves concurrent write safety with atomic write pattern (write .tmp, rename). Postgres would be more durable but adds significant infrastructure for marginal benefit — workflows are minutes-long, not mission-critical financial transactions.
alternatives: Postgres (durable, queryable, but adds infra)
Redis-only (fast but volatile, lost on restart)
SQLite (embedded DB, good compromise but different pattern from rest of forge)
decisions.resolution_notes: User override: Postgres instead of JSON files. Reasoning: forge-web is a web application, not CLI — JSON file persistence is for forge-core CLI. Web platform should use proper database. Postgres provides durability, concurrent-safe writes, queryability, and is appropriate for the web stack.

decisions.issue: forge-core integration: subprocess calls vs direct Python imports?
decisions.recommendation: Subprocess calls (py -m core.XXX) for primary integration
decisions.reasoning: Clean separation — workflow engine treats forge-core as a black box with CLI interface. Same interface used by CLI and skills. No import side effects or coupling. Subprocess overhead (~100ms per call) is negligible for workflow steps that take seconds-to-minutes. Direct imports would save latency but create tight coupling and risk import side effects.
alternatives: Direct Python import (faster but tight coupling)
REST API wrapping (over-engineering for internal modules)
Hybrid: subprocess for writes, import for reads (complex)
decisions.resolution_notes: User override: Direct Python import instead of subprocess. Reasoning: faster execution (~0ms vs ~100ms per call), tighter integration is acceptable since forge-api and forge-core are part of the same codebase. Clean API boundaries can still be maintained via module interfaces.

decisions.issue: LLM session strategy: one session per workflow vs one session per step?
decisions.recommendation: One ChatSession per step, with previous step summaries injected as context
decisions.reasoning: A full workflow (5+ steps) could consume 100K+ tokens in a single session, causing context overflow and degraded LLM performance. Per-step sessions keep context focused and manageable. Previous step outputs are injected as structured summaries (not full conversation history). This matches how forge-core works — each /next execution is independent but gets context from dependencies.
alternatives: Single session (risk of context overflow)
Shared session with pruning (complex, unpredictable)
Session per step with no cross-referencing (loses context)
decisions.resolution_notes: Per-step ChatSession accepted. Each workflow step gets its own session with previous step summaries injected as context.

decisions.issue: Concurrency model: asyncio.Task vs ThreadPool vs ProcessPool?
decisions.recommendation: asyncio.Task per WorkflowExecution with configurable max concurrency
decisions.reasoning: forge-api is fully async (FastAPI + asyncio throughout). asyncio.Tasks are lightweight, support cancellation natively, and don't hit GIL issues (I/O bound, not CPU bound). Each workflow execution runs as an asyncio.Task, with asyncio.Semaphore limiting concurrency (default 5). ThreadPool would add unnecessary complexity, ProcessPool is too heavy for this use case.
alternatives: ThreadPoolExecutor (heavier, GIL concerns for CPU work)
ProcessPool (too heavy, IPC overhead)
Celery (overkill external infrastructure)
decisions.resolution_notes: asyncio.Task per execution accepted with Semaphore(5) concurrency limit.

decisions.issue: Workflow stuck forever due to silent step failure or missing timeout
decisions.recommendation: Implement per-step timeout (default 10min) and workflow-level timeout (default 2hr) with automatic failure notification
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Per-step configurable timeout (default 600s, max 3600s). 2. Workflow-level timeout (default 7200s). 3. Periodic health check every 60s marking stale running steps as failed. 4. Automatic notification via EventBus on step failure/timeout. 5. Retry button in UI for failed steps.
decisions.resolution_notes: Risk mitigation accepted: per-step timeout (10min default), workflow timeout (2hr), periodic health check, auto-notification on failure.

decisions.issue: Complexity creep: engine grows into unmanageable mini-Temporal
decisions.recommendation: Strict phased implementation: Phase 1 linear only, Phase 2 conditionals, Phase 3 parallel. Each addition requires explicit decision record.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Phase 1: linear steps only (no branching, no parallel). 2. Phase 2: add conditional routing after Phase 1 proven. 3. Phase 3: add parallel_group after Phase 2 proven. 4. Every feature addition requires a decision (D-NNN) with justification. 5. Strict component separation (Scheduler, StateManager, StepDispatcher) to contain complexity.
decisions.resolution_notes: Risk mitigation accepted: strict phased implementation — Phase 1: linear only, Phase 2: conditionals, Phase 3: parallel.

decisions.issue: JSON file state corruption from concurrent workflow writes
decisions.recommendation: Use atomic write pattern (write .tmp then rename) with asyncio.Lock per project
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Atomic write pattern (write to .tmp file, os.rename to target) — proven in forge-core. 2. asyncio.Lock per project slug prevents concurrent writes to same workflows.json. 3. Consider per-execution JSON files (WE-001.json) instead of single file — eliminates contention entirely. 4. Backup before write (workflows.json.bak).
decisions.resolution_notes: Risk mitigation accepted. Note: with Postgres (D-002 override), JSON corruption risk is eliminated — Postgres handles concurrent writes natively. Atomic operations via database transactions.

decisions.issue: Notification persistence strategy for zero-miss guarantee (KR-4)
decisions.recommendation: Server-side notification entity (notifications.json) via existing StorageAdapter pattern
decisions.reasoning: KR-4 requires zero missed notifications and persistent notifications until resolved. Client-only storage (current notificationStore + localStorage) loses state on page refresh. Server-side entity is consistent with all other Forge entities and provides audit trail, cross-session persistence, and fetch-on-reconnect capability.
findings: Current notificationStore is client-only (Zustand) - lost on page refresh
Current NotificationCenter dismissal uses localStorage - not cross-device
Redis Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget - cannot replay missed events
JSON file storage with entity locking handles concurrent writes safely
All other Forge entities use same pattern successfully

options.name: Redis Streams
cons: Redis data loss risk without AOF

open_questions: What is the actual notification volume under concurrent workflows?
Should resolved notifications be archived after N days?

decisions.issue: Notification backend architecture - dedicated service vs event enrichment
decisions.recommendation: Dedicated NotificationService + NotificationRouter in forge-api
decisions.reasoning: Backend should own notification creation logic to ensure consistency, enable server-side persistence, and centralize event-to-notification mapping. Without a dedicated service, notification logic would be scattered across 10+ routers. The service follows the same pattern as all other Forge entity routers.
findings: Current decision notifications are created in frontend wsDispatcher - no server persistence
10+ entity routers emit events that could generate notifications
Centralizing mapping logic prevents inconsistency across frontends
Existing router pattern (CRUD + status validation) applies directly

decisions.issue: Notification UI architecture - bell enhancement vs drawer vs modals
decisions.recommendation: Enhanced NotificationCenter bell (WebSocket-driven) + response modals for blocking notifications
decisions.reasoning: KR-2 requires dedicated detail views with AI-proposed options. Modals keep users in workflow context without page navigation. Non-blocking notifications (alerts) use toast + bell count, clicking navigates to entity. Builds on existing UI components without major restructuring.
findings: Current NotificationCenter polls every 30s - easy win to convert to WebSocket
DecisionNotificationPopup already shows single-notification queue with actions
AISidebar occupies right side - drawer would compete for space
Existing entity detail pages already exist for all notification targets
4 notification types identified: decision, approval, question, alert

decisions.issue: Multi-workflow notification routing strategy (KR-3)
decisions.recommendation: Workflow metadata tagging on notifications, frontend grouping/filtering
decisions.reasoning: Adding workflow_id and workflow_step fields to notifications enables grouping without changing WebSocket infrastructure. Single WS connection per project is sufficient. Frontend wsDispatcher can group and filter by workflow context. No new channels or subscriptions needed.
findings: Single WS connection per project handles current event volume well
Separate WS channels would add connection overhead and complexity
Workflow context (execution_id, step) is available at notification creation time
Frontend already groups entities by various criteria (status, type)

decisions.issue: WebSocket reconnection gap can lose notifications during disconnect
decisions.recommendation: Implement fetch-on-reconnect from persistent notification store
decisions.mitigation_plan: On WebSocket reconnect, immediately fetch GET /notifications?status=UNREAD&since={lastEventTimestamp}. Server-side persistence ensures nothing is lost. SWR already revalidates on reconnect - same pattern. Track lastEventTimestamp in notificationStore.

decisions.issue: Notification flood under concurrent workflows causes UI performance degradation
decisions.recommendation: Server-side batching + frontend throttling + priority-based display
decisions.mitigation_plan: Three-layer mitigation: (1) Server batches notification writes (max 1 per 500ms per workflow), (2) Frontend wsDispatcher throttles notification state updates (200ms debounce, matching existing SWR pattern), (3) Priority-based display: only critical/high get popup modal, normal/low increment bell counter only. Existing toast max-3-visible pattern serves as precedent.

decisions.issue: Cross-project notification blind spot - no mechanism to notify user about other projects
decisions.recommendation: Periodic polling of unread count across all projects from NotificationCenter
decisions.mitigation_plan: Add lightweight endpoint GET /notifications/global-count that returns unread counts per project. NotificationCenter polls every 60s (same as current 30s pattern but for cross-project). Show aggregated badge on bell icon. When user navigates to project, WebSocket takes over for real-time. Global WebSocket channel is a future enhancement if polling proves insufficient.

decisions.issue: Notification type proliferation leads to growing modal maintenance burden
decisions.recommendation: Base modal component with pluggable content slots and type registry
decisions.mitigation_plan: Design NotificationResponseModal as a base component with: header (priority badge, title, timestamp), content slot (type-specific), actions slot (type-specific buttons). Each notification type registers a content renderer. Adding a new type = adding one content component, not modifying the modal system. Start with 4 types, expandable pattern.

decisions.issue: Notification data model - unified entity schema for all notification types
decisions.recommendation: Single notification entity with notification_type discriminator and polymorphic fields
decisions.reasoning: All 4 notification types (decision, approval, question, alert) share 80% of fields (id, type, priority, status, title, message, source_entity, project, workflow context, timestamps). Type-specific fields (ai_options, response) are nullable. Unified entity enables single store, single router, unified queries. PostgreSQL migration later can add JSON columns for type-specific data.
alternatives: Separate entities per type (DecisionNotification, ApprovalNotification, etc.) - rejected: 4x CRUD endpoints, 4x stores
Extend existing entities with notification fields - rejected: pollutes entity schemas, unclear ownership

decisions.issue: How to achieve >95% natural language to tool call success rate for 12 entity types (KR-1)
decisions.recommendation: Fill CRUD tool gaps (archive/remove ops, AC template CRUD) + improve tool descriptions with entity-specific examples. Measure baseline before and after.
findings: 42 tools already registered covering 12 entity types
DELETE operations missing for ALL entity types - only status-based archival
AC templates lack create/update tools - only instantiate available
Generic tools (searchEntities, getEntity, listEntities) cover basic read for all types
Scope-based filtering limits visible tools to 8-12 per context, reducing confusion
open_questions: What is the current baseline tool-call success rate?
Should DELETE tools use hard delete or archive semantics?

decisions.issue: How to implement slash command workflow sessions (KR-2)
decisions.recommendation: Leverage existing skill injection system. Frontend SlashCommandRouter maps /command to skill name + session_type. Skill SKILL.md provides step-by-step instructions to LLM. No new backend infrastructure needed.
findings: 20 slash commands already defined in SlashCommandDropdown
Slash commands currently just inject text - no workflow session triggering
Session types (chat/plan/execute/verify/compound) stored but informational only
Skill injection via @-mention already works (load SKILL.md into context)
Backend has no special routing for slash command messages
open_questions: Should workflow state tracking enforce tool ordering or just warn?
Which slash commands need dedicated workflow skills vs just text injection?

decisions.issue: How to ensure real-time UI updates <1s after AI mutations (KR-3)
decisions.recommendation: Verify existing wsDispatcher + TOOL_TO_ENTITY + SWR revalidation pipeline. Fix any mapping gaps. The infrastructure is already built and working.
findings: SWR revalidation pipeline ALREADY WORKS via wsDispatcher
TOOL_TO_ENTITY mapping routes tool names to entity types for debounced SWR mutate()
MutationTracker (5s TTL) prevents WS echo double-processing
Toast notifications (500ms debounce) already show AI updated entities
Need to verify all tool names are in TOOL_TO_ENTITY mapping

options.name: Add dedicated entity mutation events from tool handlers
options.recommendation: PARTIAL - only if Option A reveals gaps

options.name: Optimistic UI from tool result payloads

open_questions: Are all 42 tool names mapped in TOOL_TO_ENTITY?
Does backend emit entity-specific WS events (task.created) after LLM tool execution?

decisions.issue: How to maintain multi-turn conversation context without exceeding token limits (KR-4)
decisions.recommendation: Hybrid approach: pin last N tool results + auto-generated session summary, sliding window for conversation messages. Configurable budget (default 30k tokens for history).
findings: Sessions persist in Redis (24h TTL) with all messages stored
No token budget management exists - sessions will hit context limits
Agent loop has max_total_tokens (50k) but this is per-request, not cumulative
Tool results add significant token overhead per turn
Skill context budget (30k chars) already enforced but history is not

options.name: Hybrid: pin tool results + sliding window

open_questions: What is the optimal number of tool results to pin?
Should session summaries be auto-generated or manual?

decisions.issue: Feasibility assessment for O-003 - Full Entity Management via AI Sidebar
decisions.recommendation: CONDITIONAL GO. Average 3.8/5 across 10 dimensions. No hard binding constraints. Conditions: (1) measure baseline tool-call accuracy, (2) verify entity WS event emission.
findings: Technical: 4/5 - 42 tools exist, SWR pipeline works, skill injection built
Resource: 4/5 - single developer, all code in-house
Knowledge: 4/5 - existing patterns well-established
Organizational: 5/5 - solo developer, no coordination needed
Temporal: 3/5 - medium appetite realistic for incremental, tight for 95% target
Compositional: 4/5 - integration patterns proven, gaps are additive
Economic: 4/5 - marginal cost small, sidebar already exists
Scale: 4/5 - Redis/SWR handle reasonable scale
Cognitive: 3/5 - 42 tools + workflows = complex but manageable incrementally
Dependency: 3/5 - LLM tool-calling quality is external dependency (soft binding)
Average: 3.8/5 -> CONDITIONAL GO

decisions.issue: LLM tool confusion - wrong tool selection with 42+ tools in registry
decisions.recommendation: Rely on scope-based filtering (limits to 8-12 visible tools per context) + improve tool descriptions to be more distinct. Add entity-type confirmation for destructive operations.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Scope filtering already limits visible tools. 2. Improve tool descriptions with entity-specific keywords. 3. Add confirmation step for mutations. 4. Measure success rate to quantify actual risk.

decisions.issue: Data loss from DELETE/archive operations triggered by imprecise natural language
decisions.recommendation: Use archive/status-change semantics instead of hard DELETE. Add two-step confirmation: LLM describes what it will modify, waits for user confirmation. Soft-delete with recovery period.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Implement archive semantics, not hard DELETE (ADR-5). 2. Tool descriptions mandate confirmation before archival. 3. All archives are reversible. 4. Existing audit trail (changes.json) preserves history.

decisions.issue: Context window exhaustion in multi-turn workflow sessions
decisions.recommendation: Implement hybrid context management (ADR-3): pin tool results + session summary, sliding window for conversation. Set token budget per session with user-visible counter.
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Hybrid pinning (ADR-3) keeps workflow state while managing budget. 2. Token counter visible to user. 3. Auto-offer 'new session with summary' at 80% budget. 4. Configurable budget (default 30k tokens for history).

decisions.issue: Slash Command Router - frontend mapping of /command to skill + session metadata
decisions.recommendation: Implement SlashCommandRouter in forge-web that maps each /command to: skill name (for SKILL.md injection), session_type, and optional initial_hint. Reuses existing skill injection infrastructure.
decisions.reasoning: Existing @-mention skill injection already loads SKILL.md into LLM context. Slash commands should reuse this mechanism. Frontend already detects slash commands (SlashCommandDropdown). Adding a mapping layer is minimal effort with maximum reuse.
alternatives: Backend command parser (rejected: duplicates FE detection, couples parsing to BE)
Hard-coded per-command endpoints (rejected: prevents extensibility)
No special handling - just text injection (rejected: no workflow guidance for LLM)

decisions.issue: Workflow State Machine - session-level tracking for multi-step workflows
decisions.recommendation: Add workflow_state to ChatSession: {workflow_id, current_step, completed_steps[], expected_tools[], allow_deviation}. Agent loop checks expected_tools before execution. Deviation triggers warning, not hard block.
decisions.reasoning: LLMs are not state machines and skip steps. But hard blocking prevents legitimate creativity and error recovery. Soft enforcement (warn + audit) balances reliability with flexibility.
alternatives: Hard state machine blocking (rejected: prevents LLM creativity and error recovery)
No enforcement, rely on skill instructions (rejected: LLMs demonstrably skip steps)
Client-side step tracking (rejected: cannot enforce at tool execution level)

decisions.issue: Context Window Manager - hybrid pinning strategy for multi-turn sessions
decisions.recommendation: Pin last 5 tool results + auto-generated session summary (every 10 messages). Sliding window for conversation. Total history budget: 30k tokens configurable.
decisions.reasoning: Pure sliding window loses workflow state. LLM summarization adds latency and cost. Hybrid approach preserves tool results (the core workflow state) while managing budget predictably. Simple rules, no AI-based importance scoring.
alternatives: Pure sliding window (rejected: loses workflow context from early tool calls)
LLM summarization per turn (rejected: added latency and cost, quality uncertain)
Pin all tool results (rejected: no savings in tool-heavy workflows)

decisions.issue: Workflow store pattern: Zustand with direct state vs entity factory
decisions.recommendation: Use direct Zustand create() like executionStore, not the entity factory. Workflows are not a standard CRUD entity - they have unique lifecycle (start/pause/resume/cancel) and real-time WS event updates.

decisions.issue: Layout engine, Graph API design, and node architecture for multi-entity DAG
decisions.recommendation: elkjs + server-side Graph API + generic EntityNode with config map
findings: dagre deprecated, lacks edge routing - not suitable for multi-edge-type graphs
elkjs (1.4MB) provides layered layout + edge routing, can run in Web Worker
Server-side Graph API ensures data consistency vs client-side N-fetch aggregation
Generic EntityNode with config map follows L-001 store factory DRY pattern
Changes (357) and Lessons should be excluded from default graph view

decisions.issue: State management pattern for React Flow + SWR + Zustand integration
decisions.recommendation: SWR as single source of truth; React Flow as derived read-only view; graphStore only for UI preferences (filters, viewport)
decisions.reasoning: Three-way state sync (SWR + Zustand + React Flow) is the highest complexity risk (composite 25). L-018 shows infinite re-render risk with React context/state loops. Making SWR the sole data source and React Flow an uncontrolled derived view eliminates the sync problem entirely.
alternatives: graphStore owns nodes/edges, syncs with entity stores (rejected: dual source of truth)
React Flow controlled mode with entity stores (rejected: couples layout state to data state)

decisions.issue: elkjs execution environment: main thread vs Web Worker
decisions.recommendation: Run elkjs in Web Worker loaded from public/ directory to bypass Next.js webpack worker limitations
decisions.reasoning: elkjs layout for 100+ nodes can take 500ms-2s, freezing the UI. Next.js has no webpack worker support configured. Loading elkjs from public/ as a static file bypasses webpack entirely and avoids build configuration complexity.
alternatives: Main thread with loading spinner (rejected: 500ms+ UI freeze unacceptable)
requestIdleCallback chunked layout (rejected: complex, unreliable timing)
Webpack worker loader config (rejected: Next.js webpack customization fragile)

decisions.issue: Edge type phasing strategy for multi-entity graph
decisions.recommendation: Phase 1: 4 core edge types (depends_on, advances_kr, origin, derived_from). Phase 2: blocked_by, parent_child, linked_entity, knowledge_ref
decisions.reasoning: 31 relationship fields across 9 entity types produce 130+ edges. Implementing all at once creates visual clutter and excessive implementation scope. 4 core types cover the primary DAG structure (Objective -> Idea -> Task hierarchy + dependencies).
alternatives: All edge types at once (rejected: visual clutter, scope creep)
Only depends_on (rejected: loses the multi-entity value proposition)

decisions.issue: Graph-specific WebSocket debounce strategy
decisions.recommendation: 2-5s debounce for graph data revalidation; only structural changes (add/remove node/edge) trigger re-layout; position-only updates skip layout
decisions.reasoning: Current entity WS events debounce at 300ms. Graph re-layout is expensive (elkjs computation). Triggering re-layout on every entity status change would cause constant graph jumping. Structural changes (new task added, dependency changed) warrant re-layout; status changes only update node appearance without re-layout.
alternatives: Same 300ms debounce as entities (rejected: excessive re-layouts)
Manual refresh button only (rejected: breaks real-time expectation)

decisions.issue: elkjs layout computation may freeze UI for 100+ node graphs
decisions.recommendation: Run elkjs in Web Worker; implement fallback grid layout if worker fails
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Load elkjs in Web Worker from public/ directory. 2. Show loading skeleton during layout computation. 3. If worker crashes, fall back to simple grid layout. 4. Cache layout results for same graph structure.

decisions.issue: Three-way state sync (SWR + Zustand + React Flow) may cause infinite re-render loops
decisions.recommendation: Strict separation: SWR owns data, React Flow owns layout, graphStore owns UI preferences only
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. SWR cache is sole data source for graph nodes/edges. 2. React Flow in uncontrolled mode (no onNodesChange syncing back to store). 3. graphStore stores ONLY filters, viewport prefs, selected node ID. 4. Code review checkpoint: no useEffect that reads and writes to same state source.

decisions.issue: Visual clutter from 130+ edges across 8 relationship types
decisions.recommendation: Phase edge types, provide entity type and edge type filters, default to showing only core 4 edge types
decisions.mitigation_plan: 1. Default view shows 4 core edge types only. 2. Edge type toggles in toolbar. 3. Entity type filter hides nodes + their edges. 4. Hover on node highlights only connected edges. 5. Consider grouping by objective as collapsible clusters in future.
